lineitem.notes: Critical table with high scan frequency, benefits from columnar layout and vectorized execution. Date filters are highly selective (~3-15% of data).

orders.notes: Joined via PK-FK with lineitem and customer. Date filter is selective (~50% reduction).

customer.notes: Dimension table with selective filter on mktsegment (1/5 = 20% selectivity). Should be filtered early in join ordering.

nation.notes: Small dimension table, not accessed in sample queries but referenced by FKs.

region.notes: Tiny dimension table, not accessed in sample queries.

supplier.notes: Not accessed in sample queries but part of schema.

part.notes: Not accessed in sample queries but part of schema.

partsupp.notes: Bridge table between part and supplier, not accessed in sample queries.

joins.notes: 1:N join, orders side is large. After filtering customer by mktsegment (20% selectivity), this reduces orders input by 80%.
1:N join with average 4 lineitems per order. Lineitem date filter (l_shipdate) is independent and selective, enabling pushdown before join.

filters.notes: Date range filter: l_shipdate <= '1998-09-02' (90 days before Dec 1, 1998). Covers ~98% of TPC-H date range (1992-1998), low selectivity (~2% filtered out).
Date range start: l_shipdate >= '1994-01-01'. Anchors range filter.
Date range end: l_shipdate < '1995-01-01' (1 year range). Selects ~14% of total data (1 year out of 7).
Range predicate: 0.05 <= l_discount <= 0.07. Narrow range, assumes uniform distribution gives ~18% selectivity (0.02/0.11 range).
c_mktsegment = 'BUILDING'. TPC-H has 5 market segments, selectivity ~20%. Critical for early filtering in join ordering.
o_orderdate < '1995-03-15'. Covers orders before mid-1995, ~50% of data (assuming uniform distribution 1992-1998).
l_shipdate > '1995-03-15'. Complementary to orders filter but on different column. Filters ~50% of lineitem independently.

aggregations.notes: Low-cardinality aggregation (returnflag: R/A/N, linestatus: O/F). Excellent candidate for vectorized hash aggregation with minimal memory overhead. Pipeline breaker but small hash table fits in L1 cache.
High-cardinality aggregation on orderkey. Hash table size depends on join selectivity (~10-50MB). Pipeline breaker requiring materialization. Two-phase aggregation may help.

ordering.notes: Trivial sort on 3-4 groups after aggregation. Can be done in-place, negligible cost.
Top-10 query with LIMIT 10. Optimization opportunity: use min-heap (size 10) instead of full sort to avoid sorting hundreds of thousands of groups. Huge performance win (O(n log k) vs O(n log n)).

optimization_opportunities: Q1: Pure scan-aggregate pipeline, ideal for columnar storage with late materialization. Only 8 columns needed, avoid reading all 16 lineitem columns.
Q1: Vectorized execution with SIMD for arithmetic (l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount) * (1 + l_tax)). Expression compilation would eliminate function call overhead.
Q1: Date filter has low selectivity (~98% pass), but still worth applying to reduce downstream work. Selection vectors avoid materializing filtered data.
Q3: Critical join ordering: Filter customer first (20% selectivity), then join with orders (reduces by 80%), finally join with lineitem. Wrong order (lineitem first) would explode intermediate size.
Q3: Push date filters before joins: o_orderdate filter on orders scan, l_shipdate filter on lineitem scan. Reduces join input sizes by ~50% each.
Q3: Build hash table on filtered customer (smallest), probe with orders, then build on (customer ⋈ orders) and probe with lineitem. Two-step hash join strategy.
Q3: Top-K optimization: Replace full sort with min-heap of size 10. Avoids materializing and sorting entire aggregate result (~500K groups → 10 groups).
Q6: Highly selective multi-predicate filter (~1.5% selectivity combined). Use bloom filters or zone maps on l_shipdate to skip data blocks. SIMD for parallel filter evaluation.
Q6: Single-table scan-aggregate, fully pipelinable. No joins = no pipeline breakers beyond final aggregation. Optimal for vectorized execution.

Q6: Correlated predicates: l_shipdate range AND l_discount range AND l_quantity threshold. Reorder filter application by selectivity (date first, then discount, then quantity) to short-circuit evaluation.
General: Lineitem is hot table (3/3 queries). Columnar storage with compression (date columns, low-cardinality flags) reduces I/O by 5-10x.
General: All queries are read-only analytical workloads. No need for MVCC or write-optimized structures. Can use immutable columnar blocks.
General: Date columns (l_shipdate, o_orderdate) used in all range queries. Zone maps or min/max indexes enable data skipping at block level.
General: PK-FK joins dominate. Runtime cardinality estimation via bloom filters can detect selectivity changes and trigger re-optimization.
General: No complex expressions in WHERE clauses. Simple comparisons benefit from predicate vectorization and SIMD.
General: Aggregation functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT) are algebraic and support partial aggregation. Parallel execution with per-thread accumulators, then merge.
General: No self-joins, no subqueries, no window functions in sample queries. Query complexity is moderate, DP join ordering is feasible.
summary: This TPC-H subset exhibits classic OLAP characteristics: large fact table scans (lineitem), selective date range filters, PK-FK star joins, and low-to-medium cardinality aggregations. Q1 and Q6 are scan-heavy single-table queries ideal for vectorized columnar execution with SIMD. Q3 is a 3-way join requiring careful ordering (filter customer first by mktsegment) and offers a critical Top-K optimization opportunity (min-heap vs full sort). Lineitem dominates I/O (accessed in all queries), making columnar layout with compression and zone maps the highest-impact optimization. All queries are pipeline-friendly except for hash aggregations and the join build phases, which are unavoidable pipeline breakers but manageable with proper memory budgeting. Execution time optimization should prioritize: (1) columnar storage for lineitem, (2) predicate pushdown and bloom filters for selective joins, (3) vectorized scan-filter-aggregate pipelines, and (4) Top-K heap for Q3.
